title: The Girl Who Stopped Looking
author: Fablewick
lesson: The race is won by staying in your own lane.
summary: Maya keeps losing because she keeps watching the swimmer next to her. Her coach tries to teach her the only kind of race she can actually win.

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
Maya loved to swim. The pool had six lanes. One lane was hers.

Kick, breathe, kick. Kick, breathe, kick.

But in the next lane: splash! A faster girl.

Maya looked. Maya wobbled. Maya slowed right down.

"Watch your own lane," said Coach Ngu.

Maya tried again. Kick, breathe — peek.

The fast girl was far ahead. Maya stopped. Water went up her nose. Blegh!

"Watch your own lane," said Coach Ngu. "It's the only lane you can swim in."

Then came the big race. Six girls. Six lanes.

Maya put her face in the water. She did not look left. She did not look right.

Kick, breathe, kick. Kick, breathe, kick. My lane, my lane, my lane.

Her hand touched the wall!

"Did I win?" asked Maya.

"You came fifth," said Coach Ngu. "Now look at the clock."

Maya looked. The clock said her fastest time ever. Ever, ever, ever.

"You swam your race," said Coach Ngu. "That one is always yours."

Maya looked at her lane. Calm. Blue. Hers.

She smiled.

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Maya had been swimming since before she could remember. The pool felt like home: long and blue and quiet, with six neat lanes painted on the bottom.

Lane four was hers.

Kick, breathe, kick. Kick, breathe, kick.

The trouble was lane five.

In lane five there was almost always somebody fast. Maya could see her out of the corner of her eye: a blur of arms, a churn of white water, pulling ahead, always pulling ahead.

And every time Maya looked, something inside her went tight.

Her arms forgot their job. Her kicks turned to flutters. Water sneaked up her nose, which is the worst feeling in the whole world.

"Maya," said Coach Ngu from the deck. "What are you doing?"

"Watching lane five," said Maya, holding the wall. "To see if I'm winning."

Coach Ngu shook his head slowly, the way he did when he was saying something for the hundredth time.

"You can't win in somebody else's lane," he said. "You can only swim in yours. So watch yours."

Maya tried. She truly did. She would put her face in the cool blue water and tell herself, my lane, my lane, my lane. But the second she heard fast feet splashing on her left, her eyes slid over, all by themselves. And her race fell apart, just like always.

Then came the big meet. Six lanes. Six girls. And in the lane right next to Maya: the fastest swimmer she had ever seen.

Maya stood on the block, toes curled over the edge, shaking a little.

Coach Ngu leaned close behind her.

"I'm not asking you to win," he said quietly. "I'm asking you to swim your race. There's a difference."

The whistle blew. Maya dove.

Under the water, the world went quiet. Her lane stretched out ahead of her, blue and clear, with one painted line pointing the way.

Kick, breathe, kick.

A splash on her left — close, fast. Maya did not look. The crowd roared somewhere far above. Maya did not look.

My lane, my lane, my lane.

Her hand smacked the wall.

Maya pulled her face from the water, blinking. The fast girl was already out of the pool. Two more girls touched the wall behind her. Maya had not won.

Then Coach Ngu pointed at the big yellow clock.

Maya looked — and her mouth fell open. Her fastest time ever. Faster by two whole seconds.

"You swam your race," said Coach Ngu, soft as a secret. "That's the only race that has ever been yours."

Maya looked down at her lane. Calm again, blue again, hers.

And she smiled.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Maya had been a swimmer for as long as she could remember (which, when you are nine, feels like forever). The pool was where she felt most like herself. It was long and blue and mostly quiet, and it had six perfect lanes painted on the bottom, like a map drawn just for her arms.

Lane four was hers. Kick, breathe, kick. Kick, breathe, kick.

The trouble — and there was a trouble — was lane five.

In lane five there was almost always somebody fast. Maya could see her out of the corner of her eye: a streak of arms, a churn of kicking feet, a body cutting through the water like a small determined torpedo. Always ahead. Always pulling further ahead.

And every single time Maya saw it, something in her chest pulled tight, and her stomach forgot what it was doing.

She would slow down. She would lift her head to check. Her breathing would stumble, and a sip of pool water would sneak up her nose, which, as every swimmer knows, is the worst feeling in the world.

"Maya," said Coach Ngu from the deck. He had been a swimmer himself, a very long time ago, and he had a voice like still water. "What are you doing?"

"Watching lane five," said Maya, holding the wall, dripping.

"Why?"

"To see if I'm winning."

Coach Ngu crouched down and shook his head, slowly, the way he did when he was about to explain something for the hundredth time and didn't mind, but was maybe a little tired.

"Maya. You cannot win in somebody else's lane. You can only swim in yours. Yours is the only one you've got. So watch yours."

Maya tried. She really, truly tried. She would press her face into the cool blue water, take her first stroke, and tell herself, very firmly: my lane, my lane, my lane. Some days she made it almost a whole length before her eyes betrayed her. Then a splash would land too close on her left, and her eyes would slide sideways, all by themselves. The moment they slid, her arms went silly, her kicks turned to flutters, the fast girl pulled further ahead, and the whole thing fell apart exactly the way Coach Ngu said it would.

At the end of the month came the big meet. The whole team got bright new caps. Parents carried folding chairs and bags of orange slices. The pool deck smelled like chlorine and warm concrete and, somewhere underneath, a little bit like Maya's nerves.

Six lanes. Six girls. And in the lane right next to Maya stood the fastest swimmer she had ever seen: a tall girl from another club whose strokes were so clean they barely splashed.

Maya climbed onto her block. Her toes curled over the edge. Her knees would not stop trembling.

Coach Ngu stepped up behind her and set one steady hand on her shoulder.

"Maya," he said, in his quiet, still-water voice. "I am not going to ask you to win today. I am going to ask you to swim your race. Do you understand the difference?"

Maya nodded. She wasn't sure, yet, that she did.

"Your race is the one in your lane," he said. "Swim it well, and the rest takes care of itself."

The whistle blew. Maya dove.

Under the surface, the world went quiet, the way it always did. The pool was blue, and the painted line on the bottom ran straight ahead, pointing the way like an arrow. Maya kicked. She breathed. She kicked.

A splash landed on her left — close, fast, the tall girl pulling away. Maya felt her eyes start to slide.

My lane, she thought, on every breath. My lane. My lane. My lane.

Her eyes stayed home.

The crowd roared somewhere far above the water, but it was a far-away roar, like weather happening to someone else, and Maya was busy.

Kick, breathe, kick. My lane, my lane, my lane.

Her hand slapped the wall.

She surfaced, gasping, and wiped the water from her eyes. The tall girl was already out of the pool, hugging her parents. Two more swimmers touched the wall behind Maya. She had not won. She had not even come close.

Then Coach Ngu, standing on the deck, pointed at the big yellow clock on the far wall.

Maya looked.

The number on the clock was hers. And it was almost two whole seconds faster than she had ever swum in her life.

For a long, strange moment, Maya didn't feel happy or sad. She felt quiet, the way the pool feels when no one is in it. Smooth. Settled. Hers.

Coach Ngu crouched down by the lane rope.

"You swam your race," he said, soft as a secret. "That is the only race that has ever been yours."

Maya looked at the clock one more time. Then she looked down at her own lane. Calm again, blue again, exactly as wide and exactly as long as it had ever been.

And she smiled. A small, careful smile, the kind you keep.
